narration.default: Have you ever noticed how the little light on a phone or laptop slowly fades in, then fades out, like it's breathing? That gentle glow is everywhere — sleep buttons, mood lamps, indicator lights on speakers. Today you'll teach an LED that exact trick: fade up smoothly to full brightness, fade back down to dark, again and again. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first.

Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've seen this before: setup runs once when the program starts, and loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.

Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. We're picking pin 9 on purpose — not every pin on the Arduino can do what we're about to do. Pin 9 is one of a handful of pins with a special power, and you'll find out why in a moment.

steps.title: An LED is on or off — how do you fade?

narration.default: Stop and think for a second. digitalWrite only has two settings: high (LED bright) and low (LED dark). There's no in-between. So how do you get half-bright? Or one-tenth bright? With only on and off to work with, fading should be impossible. But your phone's breathing light proves it isn't. So what's the trick?

Here's the trick. The pin really is only on or off — but it switches between them thousands of times per second. If it's on most of the time, the LED looks bright. If it's on only a tiny fraction of the time, the LED looks dim. Your eye can't follow the flicker, so it averages everything together into one steady glow. This is called PWM, short for pulse-width modulation. There's a special block for it: analogWrite. You give it a pin number and a brightness from 0 to 255. Zero means fully off. 255 means fully on. 128 means halfway. Only PWM-capable pins like pin 9 can do this — that's why we picked it.

To fade smoothly, we don't want to jump from 0 to 255 in one go — we want to go 0, then 1, then 2, then 3, all the way up to 255. That means the program has to remember which number it's on right now, so the next loop knows to add one. A box that remembers a number for us is called a variable. Let's make one.

Open the Variables category. Click Make a Variable. Type the name brightness and click ok. A new set of orange blocks appears — these are how the program writes to the box, reads from it, and changes its value.

Drag a set brightness to block into setup, just below the pinMode. Type 0 in the slot. We start the box at 0 — the LED will be fully dark before the fade begins. We put this in setup, not loop, because we only want to start at zero once when the program turns on.

We want to fade from 0 all the way up to 255 — that's 256 brightness steps. Imagine writing 256 nearly-identical blocks by hand. Painful. Lucky for us, there's a block that says 'do this thing 256 times'. It's called repeat. You drop it once, put your action inside it, and the Arduino does the work.

steps.title: Drop the repeat block into loop

narration.default: Open the Control category. Drag a repeat block into the loop section of setup-and-loop. Change its number from 10 to 256. Anything we put inside its mouth will run 256 times before the loop wraps around.

Back to the Arduino category. Drag an analogWrite block inside the repeat. Set its pin to 9. For the value, drag the orange brightness variable block from Variables and drop it into the value slot. Now the line reads: send the current brightness number to pin 9. First time through the repeat, brightness is 0 — LED is dark. We're about to make it climb.

From the Variables category, drag a change brightness by block underneath the analogWrite, still inside the repeat. Leave the value at 1. Each time around the repeat, this block adds one to the box. So the brightness climbs: 0, then 1, then 2, then 3, all the way up to 255.

steps.title: A tiny pause so we can see it

narration.default: Drag a delay block underneath the change-by block, still inside the repeat. Set it to 8 milliseconds. Why so small? The Arduino is fast — without a pause, all 256 brightness steps would finish in a blink and we'd see nothing. 8 milliseconds per step times 256 steps gives roughly 2 seconds for a smooth fade-in. Just slow enough for our eyes to catch the climb.

We've taught the LED to climb from dark to full bright. But for it to breathe, it has to come back down too. So we'll add a second repeat right after the first — same idea, just walking the brightness number back down from 255 to 0. Same shape, opposite direction.

Drag another repeat block into loop, just below the first repeat. Change its number to 256 as well. We're going to fill its mouth with the same kinds of blocks as before, but with one important change in the third one.

steps.title: Send brightness to the LED again

narration.default: Drag another analogWrite block inside the new repeat. Set pin to 9. Drop the brightness variable into the value slot, just like before. When this second repeat starts, brightness will already be at 255 from the end of the fade-up — so the first thing this block does is shine the LED at full brightness.

Drag a change brightness by block underneath the analogWrite. This time, change the value from 1 to negative 1. Just type a minus sign before the 1. Adding negative one is the same as subtracting one — so the brightness box now counts down: 255, then 254, then 253, all the way back to 0.

Drag one more delay block underneath, still inside the second repeat. Set it to 8 milliseconds, just like before. Same reason — give your eyes time to follow the fade. Now the loop will fade up for about 2 seconds, then fade down for about 2 seconds, then wrap around and do it forever. The blocks are done — let's plug in an LED.

Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LED. In the pin dialog, choose pin 9 and click Save. The virtual LED appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same PWM pin your code is feeding numbers to.
Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.

Look at the LED in the simulator. It glows from dark, climbs slowly to fully bright, then fades back down to dark, over and over. No flicker. No steps you can see. Just a calm, breathing glow — even though the pin underneath is only ever fully on or fully off, switching between them faster than your eye can catch.

Five minutes ago, fading an LED looked impossible. Now it's easy. You learned PWM — the trick of pulsing a pin so fast that on and off blur into in-between brightness, controlled with analogWrite from 0 to 255. You learned variables — a named box the program uses to remember a number across loops. You learned the repeat block — say it once, the Arduino does it many times. And you learned fade math — climb the number up, then walk it back down, and what you get is a smooth breathing glow. PWM is everywhere: phone screen brightness, RGB lights, fan speed control, motor speed in toys and drones — all the same trick you just used.
